reviews.review_msg: overall good prof. marking was a little slow and not by her own fault the TAs were pretty useless. exams were hard but pretty fair. good OH, solid lectures, and pretty good assignments.

Jocelyn Minns was the last second substitute for CMPT 225 and taught the course for the first time when I had her. She made very clear powerpoints and her lectures were absolute amazing. She would go in depth on some practice problems which really helped. Exams were very fair and grading wasn't too tough. By far my favourite CS prof.

Jocelynn was a pleasure to have as a prof. She went out of her way to help me with tough concepts, walk through dense code and nail down concepts, often outside of normal office hours, never making me feel like my questions were stupid even when they probably were. HIGHLY recommend!

I am not sure if Jocelyn has a permanent position at SFU but I really hope she is given one. You can tell she cares about students and is passionate about teaching as she gives students manageable expectations about assignments and exams. I hope to see more new professors like her in the comp sci department who actually want to see students succeed
Great prof, One of the best

A bit disorganized, but passionate and very kind. He relied on the fact that students were supposed to read textbooks, but as long as I followed the readings, his explanations and assignments were very clear. Final exam ressembles much of what had been during the term.
Terrible prof
This guy is awesome
really boring class, really boring lectures. he doesnt explain concepts and terms in a thorough way. Instead of defining concepts, he just gives very incomprehensive examples. really bothers me
no study guide, no review, tons of work needed to do. I was in his cmpt 120 class, he was nice before. Now he is really bad. Jerry, seriously..I would like you to be a little more helpful.
I used to like computer science. Did terrible on midterm.  Even doing all the homework questions or understanding all the textbook examples did not help at all.
run while u can

Good person with zero skills in teaching. Assignments and labs were hard and intricate, while exams were a little bit complicatedf. The course is boring by itself, but Golnar makes every lecture the most boring event on the Earth.

To do the labs you have to understand her weird logic, taking you 40 minutes, and then write 5 lines of code in the right place The assignments were brutal, as not only you had to understand her logic, but also make your code compatible with hers The exams were ridiculous, as she only said "All questions have equal probability to be on the exam"

Yes, she's a new professor with a few hiccups along the way, but she always fixed them and seemed to really care about the class. Final class avg was B-, and a reasonable grade distribution, so read the emotional reviews here with a grain of salt. If you paid attention in class and read the text book, the tutorials and assignments were good.

Final is unreasonable. Too many coding question, I even don't know whether TAs and prof could give grade fair. There's too many codes to read and everyone's answer are completely different. The assignments are super hard. we usually spent over 20 hours in lab to finish one assignment. However, Prof is very nice and friendly in office hour.

Final exam had 5-6 C++ coding questions and it was super hard and very unrelated to what we've been studying. Not related to labs or assignments or lectures. They were just random coding questions that you cannot prepare for.

Apparently people say it's her first time teaching. She's improved over the semester, but she's still got many problems. Her assignments are difficult in both completing them, and understanding what she wants. The final exam was much harder than her midterms. She likes to use Microsoft Paint or a sheet of paper at a 45 degree angle for messy notes.

You'll get used to it but that's not necessarily a good thing. I felt like she went easy on us this semester since she was pretty new. Anyone after us should expect to be decently challenged, since the course content isn't all that hard you guy'll probably do fine. Put a little effort in to the course and you could pass with a rock teaching you.
4 or 5 written code for Final Exam.

She is not good, her lectures are not useful for learning stuffs and those are just copy of textbook, there is few thing we can learn from the materials of her lecture. In her mind, she thinks almost everyone of us are really good at c++, actually we are not. Her assignments are heavy. Try to avoid her course!

the lectures were alright but given the prior rating they weren't that amazing too. Many times while explaining he would say "you would know"made me feel a little less likely to ask more questions cause I wouldn't usually know the things already. The assignments were easy. The final project however was very hard. Midterm & final were fair.

Igor's lectures were great and he explained the concepts very well. The final project was very difficult, so it's a good idea to start on it early. The tests were a bit difficult, but fair. He also provides you with lots of practice problems which help a lot when studying for the tests.

good prof, fair exams, fair assignments, and very difficult final project. 

but be weary because there was no curve.

Igor is one of the best computer science professors at the school. he responds to messages fast, his assignments are fair, and his midterm and final are very similar to the practice. 

the only thing is, his final project was VERY hard, so make sure to start at least  a week before. 

also his course is in java so it's already 10 times easier.
While there are many topics and the final project is pretty tough, professor Shinkar is extremely fair and helpful. Would def recommend

Igor's getting a lot of hate, but I would describe him as very fair. Extremely fairly graded regular assignments, reasonable difficulty on midterm & final. Final project was hard - definitely harder than most stuff - but Igor is very helpful & active on piazza. Don't expect to be able to fully coast with him, just put in the work and you're fine.

This was a solid course. The work was fair and a lot of time was given for the project, which while difficult as long as you put in effort you got s good mark (even if you couldn't fully solve it). The tests were fair and covered topics he layed out pretty clearly on the online class forum.

He cares about his students, EXTREMELY fair and understanding. I'd take all my courses with him if I could. 

He can understand if you've genuinely studied/attempted something and gives you marks based on that as well as having the correct solution. 

project was a difficult but there are people that solved less than 25% and still got 75%+

Decent professor, but beware Igor is slightly overrated or has decreased in teaching quality. Exams are fair and reasonable. Lectures and course in general felt poorly planned, a few last minute cancelled lectures. Final project was hilariously difficult but at least was marked lightly. Definitely not a bad professor, but not an amazing one.
one of the best profs I've had over the years. super caring and understanding towards students.

Great prof and a really caring person. Allowed for resubmissions and was very lenient with his marking, his lectures weren't the best but then again I just used youtube to supplement my learning and it worked out great for his midterms and finals.

Igor is an amazing professor! His lectures are great and is very understanding and caring. His exams were a bit tough but he was also lenient with his marking. The project was also stressful but again, very lenient with the marking. Overall, W course would take again.

Igor is the best CS prof hands down. This prof is a tiny bit disorganised, but I would say that most of his lectures were clear. Final project was definitely a bit tough, but his exams were super fair (and he was able to accommodate me taking it later). Overall, if you put the effort into this class you can do super well. Thank you for the sem!

Igor was a solid prof. He was rusty after teaching this course in 2023, but the midterm and final were fair, lectures were rushed later in the semester due to him missing some days. My advice would be to do things much ahead of time as many people had issues with the project, but if you give yourself a lot of time and do research you can succeed.

Although the final project was difficult it helps you prepare for the final exam, his exams were very fair and he genuinely wants his students to succeed. Highly recommend taking it with him! Thanks Igor bless you.

If this is your first time using Java, you may find the beginning challenging. Assignment 1 was harder than I expected, and the lectures move quickly. However, after initial adjustment, the course becomes very manageable. I HIGHLY recommend going to his office hours—he is extremely helpful and will do everything he can to support your understanding
Wish he gave more practice problems that actually resembled the exam rather than telling us to go on leetcode and to mess around and waste time.
I wish I could go back in time and tell my younger self NOT to major in Computer Science.

Very honest rating, he wasn't bad enough to be < 3, but also doesn't reflect his rating of 4.7/5 when I signed up for his course. Maybe it was true that he was 4.7/5 quality years back. The project was really rough. Final exam was overall ok, some questions were heavily based off leetcode mediums, so make sure to do some leetcode.

I would have taken this class with Dr. Mitchell last spring if I have the pre-requisite for this course. My friend scored a high grade with Professor Mitchell. Final Exam was easy mainly based on lecture. So sad, he retired too early. Same course but different level of difficulty with different professors, it seems so unfair.

other students are acting like he was the worst prof at sfu. easy assignments, fair midterm, easy final, good lectures. the one thing I didn't like is how disorganized the project was. other than that, great professor.
He's nowhere near as bad as many other profs in cs (looking at you Janice), but he seems highly overrated or at least has not lived up to the teaching quality he supposedly used to have
He was alright, not awful (though the project was absurdly difficult) but he's far from being an exceptional prof either (don't know why so many say he's like the best ever he's alright)

Igor's a decent guy from all my interactions with him in person and on piazza, but he just hasn't been very good at teaching this semester. Heard he took a break and unfortunately it shows. He seemed quite rusty and the course was messy for most of the semester. Final project was crazy

Some students are so salty they are literally patrolling this RMP and giving a downvote to any positive review they see instead of studying. Emotional reviews should all be removed. He was solid and taught everything needed to succeed, and don't take my word for it this is objectively true since our midterm was almost a perfect bell curve.

This prof ain't it. Nothing against him as a person but this semester was quite of a mess on his part, especially with the final project. Many students who solved all test cases say people complaining are just vibe coders, yet most of them did not even use BFS/DFS, A*, or even IDA* like required. Igor himself probably can't solve it with those.

He's not a bad prof but also doesn't stand out to me as an exceptional one either. Good guy but his style of teaching especially how rushed the beginning was explaining java screwed a lot of ppl over. If you already have experience in java/basic data structure you'll likely be fine, if not you might wanna study up beforehand.

Final project is difficult, no one knew what was going on (Including him). From what I heard it seems like he hasn't taught in 2 years, so either he got lobotomized during that period of time or just decided he doesn't care anymore. Communicates fairly often on Piazza, but the quality of his responses were extremely questionable (project).
absolutely awful, do not take. He's is completely out of touch with reality

The midterm and assignments were easy. But the project was torture. His idea was to let us exercise A* algorithm, but everyone I knew who passed all the tests did not use A*. ppl referenced to Kociemba's or via beginner's method. Hence I don't really know what's the purpose with the project anyways. idek whether he's able to solve it via A*.

He's very fair,
- one of the highest midterm averages for this course
- poor choice of words for some questions but makes up for it by being overly generous while marking
- literally free 20% for hw, lets you attempt multiple times if you mess up
- hard project but again, hes very generous with marking so as long as you attempt and try, youre good

Rushed through core OOP concepts in the beginning so prepare to self study. midterm was challenging but fair. Active on piazza was a plus. Final project was unreasonably difficult. I think my hairline receded 4 inches coz of it
The project makes me want to jump out the window. I started early, but I'm making no real progress. Feels like I've wasted so much time for nothing

Igor's taken a near 2-year break from teaching and it was really showing. It felt like he wasn't all that interested in what he was actually teaching us, kinda meandered around for half the semester. Course content was nicely organized for each week yet the course still seemed like extremely messy. Final project was horrific

I found lectures overall to be pretty decent, wish he spent more time on more important subjects. Project felt quite unfitting and difficult to do with the things we learned in class. Entire course structure felt quite messy compared to other CMPT courses I've taken, but nonetheless still manageable.

Igor means well but I really wish he covered OOP more at the start rather than rushing past it and some core parts of Java as all the courses I've taken here have used different procedural languages. Also the final project seemed weirdly narrow, not requiring us to use most of our knowledge learned but just focus on one very difficult niche part.
Very accessible and replies very quickly on piazza. The final project was very difficult and my final solution did not engage with course curriculum as much as hoped.

Disclaimer: my semester was the first time in 2 years the prof had taught, and I was not familiar with OOP. 

I think he's an alright professor who did the best he could coming from a 2 year hiatus of teaching. While I struggled a bit in this course, he responds to questions and requests quickly, and was engaging to converse with in office hours
Igor has been nothing but an awful prof this entire term, do NOT take him EVER. He completely changed from how he was in 125

Some reviewers are acting so smug like everyone complaining are just bad students. I was excited to have Igor as a prof cause of all the praise I heard but this semester 225 has been a mess. It's a difficult course regardless, but Igor makes it way harder than it should be with his strange course decisions that have consistently made things worse.

> cs students given a whole month to do project
> Students wait until the last 5 days to do it
> Get completely filtered by the problem 
> cry and rate a 1.0 on rmp

Many such cases.

Lotta bad students here complaining even though Igor has been nothing but a saint. He's been supportive and kind all semester with an easy midterm and HW assignments. I'm not gonna say the project was easy, but it wasn't hard either. Very fair considering the time we were given. Most of the class are vibe coders, so lots crying right now lol.
this might sound harsh but at times igor reminded me of janice reagan

People gonna say i'm just a bad student or skill diff or something, but Igor expects FAR more from us than the level of the class should, especially with the final project and his bizarre illogical constraints for it. It's nonsensical how time-consuming and complicated it is with how little he bothered to explain some core concepts needed for it

I don't think his assignments are “easy” unless you use AI all the time. And he expects us to do the project without AI. The slides and lectures are organized, but if he wants us to do this project properly, I think he should've explained more of the background beforehand

He was really chill. Project was definitely hard but it is not an "unsolved" problem at all so eventually you will get the solution, it just takes time. Good lectures, easy assignments, and exams are more than fair. Take it with Igor, trust me.

i get the final project is suppose to test us and be a challenge, but not allowing the use of PDB's just makes it feel out of touch with reality. also everytime i asked for guidance on piazzo, the prof just gave me vague, incoherent respones, i didnt want full answers obviously, but even basic questions he was evasive and confusing

Probably gonna get a lot of downvotes for this as i'm in the minority, but it kind of felt like Igor was phoning it in a bit for 225 this semester. He's cancelled multiple lectures day-of, rushed through important concepts earlier this semester, and the final project is ridiculously overcomplicated and is expected to be implemented impractically.
I won't take his class again. Project was nightmare
he is amazing, I would only take him if i could
best prof ever

amazing class amazing prof. He delivered it well for a tough class, and his office hours were extremely helpful as he cares for all his students as long as they put in the time and care. (end of discussion)

Great professor. 
Pros: Clear grading criteria, fair exams, very fair/easy homework (should be free 20%).
Neutral: Final Project was 'hard' at the start but most of the class got it at the end (Fifteenpuzzle solver) so start it early
Cons: Lectures got boring and a bit fast-paced by the time the midterm passed (not necessarily his fault).
One of the worst profs I've had at SFU. Lectures were confusing, questions were brushed off, and the course felt disorganized from start to finish. Would not recommend taking CMPT 225 with her.
Nah bro all the old 1 star reviews are insane. Pure copium from people who either aren't cut out for CS or are just mad bc of a bad score lol

Anne is an amazing lecturer and honestly has some of the most organized slides out of any CS prof I've had so far. People sometimes complain about her quizzes being tough, but they're fair enough if you study and truly understand. She just doesn't hold your hand in the sense
No partial marks

She tries her best to make lectures enjoyable and listens to students feedback. She used to not post recordings of lectures but started in the middle of the semester. Her tests are reasonable and relate somewhat to the practice mt, lectures, and assignments. Some of the instructions for the assignments weren't super clear though

Anne was good professor and taught the concepts well, however, her exams and grading was not fair at all. She would not give part marks and cut marks for not writing comments in code. A few little stupid mistakes in midterms can cause a fail. So if you're someone like me who makes those tiny stupid mistakes, I'd suggest not taking her.

The concepts in this course are pretty straightforward. Her tests are brutal, though, and say bye to partial marks. She won't at all allow cheat sheets. Her assignments are straightforward. Memorize the implementation of the code as they are similar in the exam. She holds the attendance activity once a week. Her lectures don't help much for 225.

Anne is a strong lecturer such that attending lectures and following along her posted lecture slides were sufficient to learn the expected material. No textbook needed. She can be overambitious on exams, and ask too many questions or too ambiguous questions. Personally, I could do without ambiguous questions but other than that she was a good prof.

Anne is a decent teacher. However, her strict rules, general demeanour, and lack of understanding to students makes me never want to have a class with her again. She is very dismissive/argumentative if you don't say the right things. I was under constant stress because the exams were graded very harshly and I was always worried about passing.
Great lectures but exams are tricky, need to know every concept in depth to answer the questions. Overall a great teacher.

Very good lectures, she taught all the concepts really well and easy to understand. The assignments are directly related to the concepts and exams are concept focused that align with the practices done in class. If you can do the optional hw and simple linked lists operations this class will be a breeze.

She's passionate, she's goofy, best cmpt prof ever. You can tell she loves teaching and she tried hard to explain content to us. Assignments are very helpful and the exams are fair(don't listen to the other reviews, I didn't do the best but I'm not salty). She even  listens to feedback and started recording lectures.
slightly unhinged, good lectures, test and assignment heavy, use the free peer tutors!

she speaks slow but she cover a lot of material very fast which makes it really difficult for students to learns such important concepts. Students who enroll in this course have just entered their second year of their degree. Her breakdown of three of 50 minute lectures on all different days makes it really hard for students to cover their material
Good lecturer but a tough grader.

DO NOT BE DECEIVED BY THE PEOPLE SAYING NO MIDTERMS / FINAL. Her quizzes are aids. Her MCQs are notoriously difficult and are made to test you on random sentences said in lectures, not actually assessing your learning as a whole. I got above average, but thats only because I got lucky on guessing her MCQs.

Worst class I have taken thus far, learned extremely little from the class, the quizzes have very cringe trick questions, the assignment feedback is utterly useless and the assignments themselves take much longer than they should for how much you are actually learning/practicing. Severely doubt other professor is worse, so i recommend them instead.

The concepts taught were all fairly easy, mostly review from 125. Homework's took some time, but were again fairly simple as she basically gives us the template for how to do it. The only hard part about the class was the quizzes, which were 70%.

Lectures were fine, quizzes were not so good. Heavily weighted (70%) and was very tricky to get around. Class average was around mid 60s. Documentation on assignments weren't good, sometimes confusing. Quizzes had a chunk of MCQ worth 2 points each. Highly would not recommend imo.

Bad marking scheme. 70% grade quizzes, 30% grade assignments. No scaling or curving. Her "quizzes" are midterms, & awful. The assignments were reasonable & tested understanding of important concepts. I wish I could say the same for her quizzes, GIVE US FULL 50 MINUTES! She's a good lecturer, records her lectures. Study slides & in class activities.
The quizzes are basically midterms and it is suicide. Do yourself a favour and take it with other profs.
DO NOT TAKE THE COURSE WITH HER, hows that?
Just dont take it w her

Assessments have harsh grading criteria, her "quizzes," are just midterms(don't be fooled by no Midterm and Final). Her HW's are bad but the grading criteria are too specific(lost half a point because I missed a star in my print()). The class is recorded, content is not too horrible but her grading criteria make her class a 1/5 for me(and others).

Pros:She does well in teaching the material and promptly uploads lecture material. Labs can be done at home, non-mandatory. 
Cons:The assignments take a long time to do, be prepared to spend time on this as the exams are heavily based on these. The 'quizzes' as she calls it, are really just midterms, and the marking and questions are quite brutal.
DO NOT BE DECEIVED BY THE "no final exam" part of her courses. Be cautious.

As a lecturer, Anne is solid. But like how many others were saying, the quizzes were pretty stupid tbh, since they were hardly about the course content, but more of comp sci altogether. You will only do well, if you did well in 125. Also the TA's were not really that helpful in the class besides helping with small stuff but were very friendly.

Anne teaches very well within class and her explanations are clear. However, her quizzes are a different story. Not many practice is given for her notoriously hard MCQ questions and she fails to prepare us for them. If you want to learn the concepts I would recommend taking 225 with her but don't expect to get a good grade.
How's that?
Terrible professor, lectures go nowhere, quizzes are graded very harshly, assignments take long to do and also graded very harshly. If I could get her fired, I would.
Teaches well but the quizzes are terrible. They are designed to fail half the class everytime.

I really hate the way she makes her quizzes and designs assignments. Her quizzes contain MCQs which might trip you up even if you understand the material because of the way they're designed and ask about assignment sometimes. Her assignments have vague and somewhat unclear instructions. Only redeeming fact is that she's good at teaching.

SHE MADE THIS A FILTER CLASS. She purposely made midterm exams mc and no part marks so that she could fail as many students and the lab exams where if your code was 99% correct but didn't compile you would automatically get 17%. I forgot to use unsigned int instead of int for one of the lab exams and my output was what she asked but still got 35%.

This professor is so bad he made me want to transfer out of computing science. The book he recommends only teaches either in one language. He barely references the book. His notes are complete and utter garbage; completely useless. He cancels class frequently and more than half of the stuff on his final wasn't even covered in class! Please AVOID.

Arrogant prof with a horrible lecture style. He cancels his lectures and office hours often and isn't available via appointment. Best advice I can give: just stick with Greg Mori for 225 and you will be golden

This is an excerpt from his 100% official school website: "if we are chatting, and there is a q, i will limit your time to 10 mins. if you still need time after this, you can rejoin the end of the q / come to another office hr / make an appointment / see the ta." He does not conduct himself in a professional manner and continually makes blunders.

he is very terrible ! he does not use any books, and you hardly to follow him with his notes. Some notes he has written is difficult to understand such as give you some letters among a word. you do not know what he has written, and you have to spend time to find the meaning. His words are small enough that you can not see in class.
very good course. Clear and patient to explain my question. I got A+ and my final exam is over 100%. Very easy.
Worse professor with all fake accent, very confused and don't know what to do during lectures.

The worst part about this class isn't that she makes the assignments hard in nature. The difficulty lies in trying to make my code compatible with hers and trying to understand methods beyond our level in an entry level C++ course. Her labs are as long as real assignments and her real assignments incorporate way too much. I have other courses too.

Got a decent grade without much effort because all the quizzes were online , assignments were easy and mid term was basically the last semester mid term with values changed . There is just one problem with the course which is a mcq based final exam , you can loose a lot of marks there . Other than that chill prof and class

Absolutely loved him. Fair grading, excellent at explaining. He tries to be funny but he sometimes starts giggling instead LOL. I literally watched all of the lectures starting from week 3 onwards online instead of attending the lectures and I wish I did that from the first week. He is very clear at what he is teaching. TAs and assignments were OK

Toby's lectures were pretty clear, he explains things simply and straightforwardly. He also posted video recordings of the lectures which was really nice. The midterm was insanely easy, the final was tough. Make sure you can do multiple choice. Exams reflect what was taught in class, assignments were fine. Overall solid prof.

exams are pretty easy especially if you pay attention to what he says in class and know the quiz questions. also he is good at explaining concepts and mostly clear about expectations. only complaint is he's kinda weird about answering questions in class/office hours
Toby is the GOAT, amazing lectures, practical teaching, he explains very well, exams were fair, he made the course easy

He's big on C++, so if you don't know it, then get ready for lots of work. He tried hard to make the class do-able for non-C++ students, but I still found it difficult. It was his first time teaching 225 (Fall 2015). 225 shouldn't be difficult if you study well. Textbook is your friend. And GO TO HIS OFFICE HOURS! He'll help you.

A great instructor - keeps class interested, hilarious if you share his sense of humour. Assignments are a bit long, but not at all difficult if you start early. Negatives: goes on tangents when students ask questions, could give more review material. Assignments took forever to mark, but fast exam marking more than compensated.
I do suggest you to take his course. He is really patient and nice. you will get everything you need to know about cmpt225 after taken his course.

nice guy, however, speaks not clear enough and give lectures without interacting with students. However, he seems really care. If u ask him a question, he will explain to u in detail. I say, attitude counts too.

His lectures aren't that great, but he is fairly helpful in terms of questions/deadlines/marking schemes. Also, the material he presents in his powerpoint slides covers the course well enough to get a good grade on both assignments and the exams.
This guy needs to learn to speak english.
He is amazing

Pooya is one of the most generous and caring profs I've had. He's always willing to take feedback and is considerate when it comes to extending deadlines if most students are struggling with other exams. I wish all profs care about their students as much as he does

Pooya is an absolute gem of a teacher. Possibly one of the most caring and understanding professors I have had. He tries his hardest to help his students succeed, and help them understand the material. He comes across as one of the nicest professors I have ever had, and I would take any future classes with him.

Tests n Final was easy lectures were dry and unclear yet tests r based off the slides. Assignments would be handed out late with no instructions, took 3 days to update the instructions to something half bad, leaving u 2 days to finish the assignment and don't expect to get marks back on time Assignment 1 part b was released right after the final XD
I got an A but this guy is doomed. How did he even had a teaching career?!!!

Boring lecture style, but they can be helpful. Lecture slides are handwritten and barely readable. Assignment instructions were never clear and always had to update instructions because even TAs were confused. False promises on test prep and solutions. Test difficulty and grading standard were on the lighter side. Nice guy overall
ending his career on a bad note

I think it was his last semester but if you ever had to take him dont. Not only he didnt curve but he scaled down, his exams were easy but whats the point if i get 87% in the course and end up with and B+.

Worst prof ive ever taken. He is super slow in grading. Doesnt bother to answer any questions and he acts like he is the smartest person in the room. His notes have issues in them but he doesnt bother to fix them before releasing them. He also scaled down, making everyone's grade much worse than it should be. How exams seem easy, but grading bs.

I hate courses where all you do is stare at the lecture slides. No practice no nothing. The way he teaches CS is archaic, should utilize more practice and coding on a computer e.g leetcode style, while paper tests should only be reserved for stuff like big-O and similar. Dunno if it's the problem with him or SFU CS tho. Horribly late grading btw.
Morally bankrupt, maybe even evil.

David is the perfect example of everything that is wrong with CS professors at SFU. David makes ~161K annually paid by us students ofc and yet he doesn't bother to answer our questions or put out any of the content he has promised his students until it's too late. For example, he released the final exam review, a day before the exam.

He encouraged the use of the online forum yet ignores all important questions. Don't bother asking about the promised exam review, he won't respond to you.Don't bother asking if we will receive grades back months later, he will simply ignore you.Your students aren't dumb it's very obvious that you ignore all the questions that ask something of you.

David began the course by setting up his canvas page about 2 weeks after classes started. One section, included textbook readings, he updated it once and never again. He was slow at getting assignments out and even slower at grading them and by slow I mean getting assignment 1 back 2 and a half months after it was due. (1)

His lecturing is basically circling his slides. His assignments are vague no clarification. His class is heavily on exams, even though the exams are ez, and you study the lecture you are fine. But then you get above 75 on the exams and you end up with a B, because he decided to change the boundary for letter grades (doesnt have on in the beginning)
he's a sweet heart, despite late grading, bro really cares about his students
handed in almost 6 assignments, but only 2 were released until the final exam date.
Released the assignment the day after it was due. Gave me back my marks for my final after I graduated 2 years later. Can't recommend David Mitchell enough 👍🏿.
This professor would give you a hard time understanding his assignment. He underperforms at writing an easy-to-understand description, always leading to confusion and sudden changes in the process.
Just like the other computer science profs. Withholds informations, teaches topics that personally interest him, and but tricky.

He is a respectable prof, he teaches with a soothing tone, easier to grasp the contexts. BUT, if you don't attend his class, even if you knew the material before, you're cooked. Because in exams he would ask you to recall what was taught in the lecture to be the standard for grading. If do can't bring it up, you're cooked (even "useless" details)
If his life depended on writing coherent instructions for assignments, he would die.

Professor Mitchell's lectures can be hit-or-miss, but using Unstuck study for extra practice really helped me stay on top of the course material. The assignments are a bit disorganized, but overall a decent class if you put in the work.
The lecture is pretty boring, hand-wrote powerpoint kinda hard to read, grading takes long time makes me feel annoying, no practise questions that can help the quizzes and tests.

Great teacher.

Wish the class had more practise homework so we dot have to search to practise data structures practise by hand. Practise midterms would help too.

David only gave back a single assignment during the semester out of four. Our second assignment was given back a day after classes had ended, with no feedback. Don't take David if you like knowing your grade.

Assignments were not graded and returned in a timely manner, syllabus was disorganized and he expects you to attend class to receive the complete lecture. He provides very little guidance on what we should scrutinize when submitting our assignments, and his TAs were as disorganized as he was. Overall a nice man, but prepare to expect the worst.

Assignments were poorly organized, causing confusion among students and constant catch-up due to inconsistent deadlines. Grading delays left us uncertain about our academic progress. Lectures lacked engagement and humor, and his tardiness disrupted classes, worsened by restrictions on photographing materials. Very superficial lectures.

Professor Mitchell's lectures and explanations in the second half of the course were confusing for me and I often had to look at other resources. Lecture slides would always be posted late. Assignments had very confusing and vague instructions. Overall, he's chill and exams are not too difficult if you understand the concepts from his slides.

David Mitchell is a great guy, his lectures are very good and he answers everyone's questions very well. The class was super easy too. However, he takes forever to mark (and assign) the assignments and the instructions are super vague on what to actually do. I don't know why everyone is hating him so much though he's pretty good overall professor.

Terrible assignment organization, distribution, doesn't zip assignment files when handing them out but expects students to zip submissions. Marks assignments way too late.

His exams are easy although he provides no practice exams.
A bit difficult to understand the explanations, but his finals and midterms are good.

Everything is in the lectures. The exams can catch you off guard, but they're not too difficult. He's a great guy, and genuinely cares about your success. Super accessible outside of class and he's pretty lenient with marking. It was a hard to focus in lectures, but everything you need to know is in the slides.
My favourite prof out of every CS Major course I took, he explains the content very well and helps as many students succeed as possible. Would take if he's an option.
She was great. Long time ago!
Wow this was ages ago. I remember it was a great course and a great prof.

She is a great instructor, her notes are clear and easy to follow. As long as you attend class and follow the examples she solves in class you should expect at least a B. Many of the questions she includes on her quizzes and tests are taken directly from class notes.
Awesome prof.
I actually remember this course quite well.  She was obviously knowledgable.  And I think this was her 1st time teaching so she wasn't 100% ready every time but good overall.
I just stumbled upon this page and remembered her.  Good course!

I took this class ages ago! I was just looking at some of my SFU prof's pages on RMP. As most of people mentioned here, the prof was very nice. Fair amount of assignments and I remember exams were similar to the problems in the assignments.
Don't remember much but a fairly easy course.
Good course.

The lectures were really helpful, and if you attend the classes and labs and do the self-quizzes and also use the achievement tracker, you can easily pass this course, but you need to put some effort. He always gives really good feedback. If you need help, ask him for a recommendation; he will help you.

Victor Cheung is a decent professor for 225. His lectures were detailed and took time explaining things. The exams were decent but he provides self-tests based on the weeks and similar samples. This course is passable but you do need to go over the slides, attend labs, complete self-tests. The assignments are well explained and not hard to complete

Dr. Cheung is great at explaining course content, and he clearly cares for the course. I personally felt that the exams were a bit of a curveball compared to lecture/assignment material; I learnt a lot, just make sure you have the time to thoroughly invest into the class. I felt the labs were not beneficial (largely a repeat of lecture slides).

125 was just a weeder course, ignore those. He's a great guy; very accessible via email and owns up to mistakes (first time teaching 225). Gives feedback for improvement with high expectations (but that's good; y'all are just soft). Fair assignments/exams covered in content with self-check quizzes. Lectures may get dry but what CS courses aren't.

not as bad as people say. Assignments were simple, exams were hard yet fair and provided resources like self tests to learn from. He expects alot from students so he does get critical when exam averages are low. 225 is an important course so if you want to do good you have to do extra studying, no slacking. Also easy to approach for questions

Why have I lost ALL my hair this semester? This was the most stress inducing class I have ever taken. He was egotistical and unorganized for exams. The slides were always incorrect; Now, I wonder if he works as a part-time mime since his hand motions have taught me more American Sign Language than Data Structures & Algorithms in 13 weeks. RUN AWAY.
at least i'm done

Vector's a great proff that actually cares about his students. Very easy to approach and ask questions. His lectures may seem very boring/repetitive however his exams were very fair and all the content was from lectures. Assignments were straightforward and you can actually learn quite a bit from them.

All the 125 reviews are right. It transferred onto this class. He mocked us for our MT average (57%), is condescending when it comes to answering questions, and clowns us when we don't know how to code certain concepts which he gave little no to guidance on. Also, half the course was boring yap abt his DSA drawings, and slides with typos
read textbook + self study > going to lecture. save your time transitting and spending 1 hour in lecture

This prof is very unorganized, there is miscommunication between the prof and the TA's, we didn't get our first assignment until a month and a half into the semester. Shermer just reads off the slides and barely shows any coding. Avoid this prof if you actually want to learn something in this course.

Tom is a pretty understanding and good prof, as he understants the struggle of remote learning and mental health. However, the assignments for his class are very difficult (theres 6) and requires the ENTIRE week to work on if you dont have a strong understanding in C++. His midterm was terrible but he made the final fairly easier for students.

Tom is very approachable for any questions over email/piazza and I appreciate that he has a sense of humour and understands that we have lives outside of school (reasonable deadlines + difficulty, etc.) Also, changing the grading criteria to favour assignments (60%) helped with the online format. The final (30%) was of a fair difficulty. Great guy!
Extremely disinterested in online courses, practically reads verbatim off the slides. Gives no information about tests

Lectures are extremely dull but are useful. Assignments are good in length and difficulty. However, he does try to cover a lot of materials. Exams contain no surprises. He really knows the subject matter, but I feel he doesn't really care too much whether students could understand his delivery. Overall not the best teacher, but far from the worst.

I have never experienced a teacher this bad in my life. How do you teach computer science without ever demonstrating a single program? No source code or example programs, only bits of pseudo code on the slides. His lectures are so dull I can barely get through the videos. His teaching leaves me more confused than I was before this class.

I got a C+, he is the reason I am not able to transfer into CMPT. that semester I took only CMPT 225, the quizzes were different from the assignments. I viewed my examed after it was marked and found several mistakes, got a 7% increase.

Seems like a nice guy but he can't teach. Just reads off the slides. Seems to have no idea how to use a computer. Assignments were basically free marks. Exams were okay. If you do better on the final than any / all of 3 the "quizzes", your final exam mark replaces those.

His lecture is useless and seldom reply email. It is CMPT225 but 50% of the content I felt like CMPT125. It is better to just reading the book and study at home. His quizzes are very boring (one question testing induction proof) and lack of marking standard. The average of quiz is about 60%. Just go for another professor...
He is just the best prof in SFU CS.
One of the best compsci profs I've encountered. Really friendly, explains topics clearly, and exams were fair. Do the work and you'll do fine.

He is a good prof but not for a CMPT course. He teaches the course like it is a social science course(He wastes too much time on slides than necessary but slides have all the knowledge you need to pass.) so attendance is not needed. Assignments took most of the grade(%60) so it is less stressful. You need to know pre-reqs because he uses them.

Assignments and exams were pretty fair and related to course material. Focused more on theory and pseudocode than actual coding which is good imo since a data structures & algorithms class should try to be language agnostic. Only major cons were posting assignments/study guides late and slow marking on assignments. Good for 1st time teaching 225.

He has a different approach to teaching computing. His assignments have a coding part and written part. Midterm and final had all written questions,mostly about coming up with algorithms, sometimes pseudocode. I liked the idea of testing us heavily on algorithms because you learn a lot of coding in other classes anyway. Slides were self-explanatory

This class in a nutshell: posts study guide Wednesday, posts practice exam Thursday, FINAL EXAM FRIDAY. He literally reads of the slides and if you're kind of new to coding have fun because he does not show any examples of how to code things. He'll give you the algorithms for some functions but you're going to have to learn the rest yourself.

Unorganized, read only from slides, didn't do any examples with class, didn't teach code other than 1st few days. Almost always away when needing to meet. Homework vague marked slowly with little feedback. Forgot to give, needed code for assignment few hours before, deadline. Gave 2 weeks for each assignment for 1st half, 2nd half was given 1 week.

He just reads the lecture slides and never does any kind of coding practice in class. His lecture slides also looks like the complex introduction of the topics that seems that he's reviewing the stuff in his 700 level cmpt classes. The assignments are totally vague. If you actually want to learn then don't take his class.

Seems extremely unorganized. Lectures often make simple concepts seem confusing. Assignments, lectures, and midterms could be mistaken for 3 different courses. Does not really teach material, instead chooses to solve problems in his head and describe his thoughts (basically trying to teach math strictly verbally)

He cares about the students and tries to give them the best he can. If you have any difficulties with his course, go to his office hours. He tries to give a full understanding of the ppt slides. At first, I won't guarantee whether you will like him or not but in the end, you won't regret taking his lecture as a lot of the students in my class did.

Nicest, kindest professor you will ever meet, Such a great guy, and always so helpful and understanding, even for assignement extensions, etc. Good lectures too, although majority of the course was just set by the John Edgar, midtern and exam were fair.
Kindest and most helpful CS prof I've had yet.
Such a great guy.... def recommend him

David is a good prof, some explanations are confusing (mostly cuz of the slides). He does his best to help. The exams were quite challenging although I understand he didn't write them. Assignments are really long and difficult. Most took 10-20 hr and there's 5. 50% final so gotta lock in for it. Final grading scheme / scaling very fair overall!
He just good

This guy is very detailed. You know exactly what to study; he even explains the code implementations. He has lecture recordings from day 1 and is genuinely an awesome guy.
He has a strong accent thogh, but it works in your favor. keeps you awake as he tries to be funny. 
in my opinion, the most goated 200 level course prof.

A really good professor, answers my questions and explains it really well so that I can understand it. He explains the slides while also giving us examples so that we understand what the slides are telling us. If he doesn't really understand what are the slides are saying, he will put the explanation in the announcement later in the day on canvas.

David is one of the BEST profs I've had. His lectures are amazing and very interesting. He is a really nice person and responds to all email fast. His exam study instructions are very clear and helpful.
Like the other commen said was help back my John's slides. However David is an amazing person and really easy to talk too. Replies to emails very quickly, some would say to quickly!

Goated prof, incredibly approachable, gives great feedback, and very helpful and understanding. Unfortunately, he was being held back by the Burnaby section's lecture and exam materials which were abysmal in quality and difficulty. If it weren't for David's input, I would have failed.

He's lecture is clear. It is important to get used to his accent. He is willing to help students. 
However, he always mark tests very late. For the midterm he released the grade after the course drop date(almost 3weeks after the test). There are plenty of online sources for this course. So the grade part is important when taking it in university.
THE GOATTTTTTTTTTTTTT 🐐 Lectures are easy to follow and he gives advice on how to perform well in class. Love this guy so much.

Dr. Chou is a great teacher, with clear instructions and very intuitive explanations;however Some of the tests are very long and need to be done very fast. Read the textbook, understand the homework completely, and really try to understand the structures and you will have an easy A+

He says he's fair and a true gem. He tries to be funny but you won't laugh when you see your grade. If you don't have the exact expected output be ready to get 0. It's pass or fail. Quoting him "excels in hardship". Be ready to be forged by the trial by FIRE.
Very nice person, but has no idea how to teach anything.

Although John's course is in-person, you probably shouldn't attend his lectures. He reads off of slides and goes on long tangents, so his lectures are empty (< 5% attendance).

Midterm average was 42%, which he bragged to his CMPT130 class about. Final was pretty okay, granted you memorized his posted notes, but the average on it was still 48%.

John is a great professor and responds to online questions extremely quickly. The content covered is difficult, and without proper studying or review it is easy to get behind. Lectures can feel bland, but if you take the time to ask him questions he is super knowledgeable and willing to help explain. Expect a hard midterm and fair final.

Johns course was pretty difficult but you learn alot from it, the exams were very legthy but were reasonable difficulty wise, same for assignments. What I like about John, is that hes actually really nice, and accessible outside of class, not just for questions, but other things, since hes a member of the undergrad chair for comp sci.

John's a really nice professor, if you actually talk to him about assignments or grading, he is usually fairly generous, his lectures were boring, but they were also really informative, quizzes and assignments were pretty fair, mostly adt implementations. Midterm and final both fair difficulty wise, but too long. TA's were really harsh markers tbh.

His lectures are really boring and the midterm was pretty bad, BUT the assignments, quizzes and final were all extremely fair. Considering that the midterm was only worth 15%, the class overall wasn't difficult. He's a nice guy too and generally pretty forgiving in my experience
Lost his will to teach about 10 years ago. Loves reading slides and making Starcraft references as much as possible. Highly recommend this class for a good nap at 8:30am.

The class itself is easy, it just has a pretty high workload. John's lectures were alright, I found that I often had to go through the recordings to connect the concepts, and this took hours. But the quizzes aren't hard, the final was fair too.

Edgar's a solid prof, fair assignments (basic ADT implementations), weekly quizzes are pretty easy, and final was straightforward. The midterm was a bit tough, but only worth a small portion of the grade, and he did scale a bit at the end. Solid choice for this course, gives a good foundation.

Pretty big yapper. Lectures are a little boring but informative. Really try your best to understand the material. Tests are just leetcode easy so you should be doing leetcode as much as possible. Assignments are were pretty easy and just ADT implementations.

The final was very fair and representative of the material and thoroughly tested your understanding. It was comprehensive yet balanced, allowing us to demonstrate our understanding and apply our knowledge meaningfully.

Professor John Edgar is a great professor. Contents on his slides are clear and strightly related to assignment/mid-term/final. No assignments or quiz are off the scope. Go start writing some C++ code before taking this course (if you don't, you will also be fine). PS: Final wieghts 50%!!!!!!!

John Edgar is an alright prof. He explains the concepts well but is pretty dull most of the time, lectures are kinda boring. Class difficulty was ok but TAs are very harsh on assignment grading. Also, the Canvas quizzes are false hope in regards to test difficulty, the tests are much harder. Also, final exam worth 50%, ouch

CMPT225 is a hard LD course, so many people will bash him for harsh exams; he was decent/fair. Exams had tricky MCQ, but doable written. Quizzes from slides, assignments were fairly straightforward. Quiet/monotone during lecture but provided great insights to material. Strongly suggest showing up to class to grasp a better understanding of DSA.
John's a chill guy.

You know, I actually really liked John as a prof, despite not liking him all that much at first. His midterm was very difficult, but only worth 15%. The assignments, quizzes, and final were all very fair and worth most of the grade. His lectures were enthusiastic and he often went on funny tangents in class lol. Very responsive to emails too

Lectures are a little boring but are recorded and slides decent. You will have to learn a good bit of implementation on your own but thats expected. Assignments (for the most part) and quizzes are fair. Prof is quite nice and replies to emails quickly. Only issue is no sample exams which made studying for them really hard.

Honestly John is a really enjoyable lecturer and in the end his grading was quite fair. The midterm was pretty tough but as it was only 15% compared to a 50% final, so it's not that hard to bounce back. Assignments were a lot of work which is a given considering the complexity of the topic. Be prepared to do a lot of coding practice.

grading criteria is clear, and he is very organized, but thats about it. HORRIBLE exams and an extremely tough grader! Quiz questions rarely even show up on actual exam material, Assignments are extremely difficult and will leave u to learn concepts that he hasn't even gone over in class with the same difficulty, he sucks, better luck next time ig

John is a great professor, he definitely likes to go on tangents though during lecture. I found his assignments and quizzes easy. His exams are fair but without example examinations can leave a lot students putting time into the wrong elements of the material. John is very approachable and ensures that grade corrections are addressed.
50% final with insanely low avg, slides are confusing af. Had to learn on my own
Good lectures, good assignments, final was very fair, midterm was bad, but other than that he was great. His grading scheme sucked, that's why I gave a 4 not a 5

His multiple choice questions on the actual exam are way more difficult than the quiz questions he assigns. He claims they're of similar difficulty, but that is not true at all. the final exam being worth 50% of your grade means no matter how well you've done throughout the semester, one exam ends up deciding whether you pass or fail.
Very vague about the final, and gives no practice leading up to the final- when students ask questions, he is very unhelpful. Do not recommend, you won't learn much.

His lecture slides are okay, but the lectures are a bit dull—he needs to make them more engaging and stop reading off slides. The tests are very hard and not really related to the material. The class average was 42%, John made the test unnecessarily long for the time given. Grind out the leetcode for this class and understand the data structures.

I would say that John is very nice and approachable, and does a decent job explaining most concepts. His assignments are mostly fair and based on the data structures we learned in class. His midterm was killer tho, but other than that, it's a good experience. Many will complain, but that's just 225, it's a hard class.

His lectures are actually pretty good, and his weekly quizzes are very fair. The assignments are also not too bad, but require some problem solving. One major con is that his midterm was way too long, causing many people to do poorly. He's approachable outside of class and during office hours.

I like the course, assignments are fun(to me) and lectures are good. But, I wish he was more invested in our success and kinder. The talk he gave us after the midterm made it seem like our fault and he basically spent the whole time trying to defend himself saying that the midterm was reasonable when it clearly was not considering everyone failed.
He couldn't do his own midterm in class, something is clearly not right with him. I BEG YOU, PLEASE AVOID:

He just yaps at lectures and tries to avoid the disappointment that is his midterm, it's like the elephant in the room, but he refuses to talk about how bad it is. His assignments and midterms suck, not a good professor tbh I'd rather take Janice.

John Edgar is an expert at making students burn out. Avoid at all costs. His assignments are way too time consuming for what little weight they contribute. Majority of people scored below 50% on the midterm. Pretty bad overall

Lectures, slides and quizzes make you think the class is easy. The assignments are terrible and are way harder + longer than they need to be. They are WAY too hard comapred to what he teaches. Some midterm question have nothing to do what he teaches and it was way longer than it shouldve been. No practice midterm made this a lot worse.

Our midterm average was brutal compared to other years—42% versus 80%, solely because of John. Please, AVOID this professor. There is no practice midterm, which is pretty unrelated to the lecture slides. Overall, the slides are pretty useless and only discuss the basics. Assignments are also complicated with few resources. AVOID!

Solid prof. Lectures are enthusiastic, he's nice and funny. His assignments are fair and doable, but require some problem solving skills. Weekly Canvas quizzes are quite straightforward and good practice. His midterm was really difficult though, mainly due to time constraints. Be prepared to write quickly. Nonetheless, he's decent.

Midterm was super unfair, completely unrelated to most of the lectures and material + 50 minutes for 15mcq and 2 hard written answers is just unfair. In addition, a lot of the assignments are unrelated to the material, and pretty hard with little guidance. John is nice, but that is pretty much the only pro.

John edgar's midterm was very unreasonable. Considering we had no cheatsheet, no sample paper and tough coding questions which require you to think critically, I would not recommend John to anybody. I know I failed miserably even though i studied quite a lot. 15 mcqs + 2 difficult coding problems in 50 minutes is unfair.

Pros: Nice guy, cracks a few jokes during lectures. Has weekly quizzes on Canvas that are pretty fair (if you are keeping up, you should get at least 7/10). Assignments are pretty fair and requirements are well-organized.


Cons: Midterm was just BAD. 10 page exam in 50 mins. 15 theory heavy MCQ (trick questions) and HARD written section.

John is a nice guy. But good god he needs to start learning how to use a microphone properly. You literally can't hear him if you are at any row other than the front few. His exams are also the dumbest thing ever - way too long and hard for a 50 minute exam. Reads off of slides during lecture in the most monotonous voice. AVOID!!

Honestly he was fine professor I thought, but his midterm was so far beyond unreasonable I'm genuinely shocked. Everyone I've talked to has said it was extremely hard and not proportional at all to lectures and assignments. There was so much on there that we didn't learn and had no idea what to study.

I would say he doesn't even care if you are listening to him or not. His mic is always turned off or doesn't work for some reason. He doesn't go much in depth compared to how difficult his exams and assignments are.
